questions.question: Why does an AP represent linear growth?
questions.answer: Because the difference between terms is constant.

questions.question: Why does an AP with d = 0 remain constant?
questions.answer: Because there is no change between terms.

questions.question: Why does a negative d produce a decreasing AP?
questions.answer: Because each term is less than the previous term.

questions.question: Why is the nth term linear in n for an AP?
questions.answer: Because it is expressed as a + (n − 1)d.

questions.question: Why does an AP graph form a straight line?
questions.answer: Because the change between terms is uniform.

questions.question: Why can an AP have fractional common difference?
questions.answer: Because d can be any real number.

questions.question: Why is the nth term formula invalid for non-AP sequences?
questions.answer: Because the difference is not constant.

questions.question: Why is an AP suitable for modelling salary increments?
questions.answer: Because increments are usually fixed each year.

questions.question: Why is the first term important in an AP?
questions.answer: Because it determines all other terms.

questions.question: Why is the common difference important in an AP?
questions.answer: Because it controls the rate of change.

questions.question: Why does changing d change the slope of the AP graph?
questions.answer: Because d represents the rate of increase or decrease.

questions.question: Why does a larger d make terms increase faster?
questions.answer: Because the difference added each time is larger.

questions.question: Why does an AP with a negative first term become positive later?
questions.answer: Because repeated addition of positive d increases terms.

questions.question: Why does an infinite AP not have a last term?
questions.answer: Because the sequence never ends.

questions.question: Why does the nth term formula require both a and d?
questions.answer: Because both starting value and rate of change are needed.

questions.question: Why can two different APs have the same nth term formula?
questions.answer: Because different a and d values may simplify to same form.

questions.question: Why does AP modelling fail for exponential growth?
questions.answer: Because the change is not uniform.

questions.question: Why is AP considered a special type of sequence?
questions.answer: Because it has constant difference.

questions.question: Why does the difference between consecutive terms remain same?
questions.answer: Because of the definition of AP.

questions.question: Why is AP useful in real-life modelling?
questions.answer: Because many situations involve uniform change.

questions.question: Why does an AP always have a definite pattern?
questions.answer: Because the difference between terms is fixed.

questions.question: Why does an AP with d = 1 represent natural numbers?
questions.answer: Because each term increases by one.

questions.question: Why does an AP with even numbers increase by 2?
questions.answer: Because each even number differs by 2.

questions.question: Why is AP not suitable for compound interest modelling?
questions.answer: Because compound interest grows exponentially.

questions.question: Why is understanding AP important in mathematics?
questions.answer: Because it forms the basis for studying sequences and series.
